summary: Anglo-Saxon poet, author of Caedmon's Hymn — the oldest extant piece of Old English poetry. An illiterate cow-herd at Whitby Abbey who received divine inspiration in a dream, as recorded by the Venerable Bede.

works.name: Caedmon's Hymn
works.description: Nine-line Old English poem praising God as creator. The oldest surviving Old English poem.

clues.clue: Oldest extant Old English poem — nine lines praising God as creator
examples: the oldest extant piece of Old English poetry
Nine-line verse which opens 'Now let me praise the creator of Heaven's kingdom'

clues.clue: Opens "Now we ought to praise the Guardian of the heavenly kingdom"
examples: opens by declaring 'Now we ought to praise the Guardian of the heavenly kingdom'
begins 'Now we must honour the guardian of heaven'

clues.clue: Describes heaven as "a roof" and "middle-earth" for humanity
examples: 'Heaven as a roof' and 'the middle-earth, the Guardian of mankind'
creating 'for the children of earth / heaven as a roof,' and 'then middle-earth'

clues.clue: Composed in a dream vision while sleeping in a stable
examples: this poet composed his most famous work in a dream vision while sleeping in a stable
a figure in his dream told him 'you can sing for me . . . Sing me creation'

clues.clue: Survives only in copies of Bede's Ecclesiastical History
examples: survived through manuscripts by this author... the Venerable Bede
survives only in copies of this medieval scholar's work Ecclesiastical History

works.name: General / Biography

clues.clue: Illiterate cow-herd at Hild of Whitby's monastery

clues.clue: A fourth major codex of Old English literature is named for him (though he wrote none of it)
examples: The fourth major codex of Old English literature is named for this poet, though he almost certainly wrote none of the poems in it

comprehensive_summary: Caedmon is clued as an Anglo-Saxon poet, author of Caedmon's Hymn, which is the oldest extant piece of Old English poetry. The nine-line poem praises God as creator, describing heaven as "a roof" and "the middle-earth" for humanity. According to the Venerable Bede's Ecclesiastical History of the English People, Caedmon was an illiterate cow-herd at Whitby Abbey under the Abbess Hild (St. Hilda). He left a banquet because he did not know how to sing, and a figure in a dream told him "Sing me creation," after which he composed the Hymn. The poem survives only in copies of Bede's History. The use of the word "scop" in the poem has led to debates about whether Caedmon is analogizing divine creation to poetry. A major codex of Old English literature is named for Caedmon, though he almost certainly wrote none of its contents. Sean Anthony argued the story of Caedmon's life was based on accounts of Muhammad by Ibn Ishaq.

cards.front: Poem: oldest extant Old English poem; nine lines opening "Now we ought to praise the Guardian of the heavenly kingdom"
Poem: composed in a dream vision while the poet slept in a stable at Whitby Abbey; survives only in copies of Bede's Ecclesiastical History
Poem: describes God creating "heaven as a roof" and "middle-earth" for humanity
